mnemonics.title: Remember All 6 Operators
mnemonics.content:
Mnemonic: 'NEGAR' = Not Equal, Greater, And, Relational
Or: 'S.S.S.S' = Same Side, Smaller Side
Operators: == (Equal), != (Not Equal), > (Greater), < (Less), >= (Greater or Equal), <= (Less or Equal)
Remember: '> points to larger value' - the arrow points to what it can be equal to or greater than.

mnemonics.title: == vs equals()
mnemonics.content:
'== checks WHERE, equals() checks WHAT'
== = wallet (same person)
equals() = looks (same appearance)
For Strings: == = address, equals() = content

acronyms.usage: Relational operators always return boolean and work with specific types (numeric only for >,<,>=,<=)

acronyms.expansion: equals() method - Quality Equals Are Reference Equal
Parentheses, Exponents, Multiplication, Division, Addition, Subtraction - Relational
acronyms.usage: In expression evaluation, relational operators come after arithmetic operators

visualTips.visualization: Draw a number line: < points left (smaller), > points right (larger). 5 > 3 means 5 is to the right of 3 on number line. The '>' symbol shape opens toward larger number.
Think of a traffic light: TRUE = Green (go), FALSE = Red (stop). Relational operators act like traffic signals for program flow.
Draw two boxes (objects) with same content but different addresses. == checks if boxes are same box (same address), not if contents match.

analogies.analogy: == is like checking if two people live at the same address. equals() is like checking if two people have the same name/appearance, regardless of where they live.
Relational operators are like judge giving verdict (true/false) based on comparison. The program then decides which code path to take based on this verdict.
Like a circuit breaker: if first switch (condition) breaks (false in AND), no need to check second switch - whole circuit is broken (false).
>= is like 'at least this much' (≥), <= is like 'at most this much' (≤). The bar represents equality, the arrow shows direction of comparison.
